questions.title: How are child events wrapped on the parent stream?
questions.context: Today coordinator.ts:1118 `teedEmit` forwards each child engine event to the parent stream verbatim — the event's `runId` is the CHILD's id. STREAM-01 says child events must be 'wrapped with `parentRunId` and the child's `runId`, so consumers can demultiplex.' The wrapping shape is the single biggest public-surface decision in this phase. Phase 1 D-04 added `sub-run-*` markers; child INNER events (model-activity, tool-activity, turn) currently bleed through as-is.

options.label: Add optional `parentRunId?: string` to every event type that can flow through a parent stream
options.description: Lightweight, additive. When a child event is forwarded by `teedEmit`, set `parentRunId = parent.runId` before re-emitting. Consumers demux via `event.parentRunId === handle.runId ? own : child`. Pros: no envelope, existing event consumers unchanged, replay path trivially preserves the field. Cons: every event type gains an optional field (subtle public-surface bloat); grandchildren need a chain (see Q-02).

options.label: Wrap child events in a new envelope: `{ type: 'sub-run-event', parentRunId, childRunId, event: <original> }`
options.description: Explicit envelope makes the demux trivial — type-discriminated. Pros: zero changes to inner event shapes, easy to filter, clean recursion (envelopes nest). Cons: BIG breaking change for existing stream consumers iterating events directly (they now see envelopes, not raw events); replay parity gets harder (trace stores raw events, stream sees envelopes); doubles the surface.

options.label: Add `parentRunIds?: readonly string[]` chain on every event (root → ... → immediate parent)
options.description: Like (a) but generalized for grandchildren. Empty/absent at root; `[parent]` at depth 1; `[grandparent, parent]` at depth N. Demux at any level via `event.parentRunIds?.[0] === handle.runId`. Pros: handles arbitrary nesting elegantly. Cons: every event gains a list field, slight serialization overhead, slightly more work for consumers vs flat parentRunId.

options.label: Don't re-emit child events on parent stream — only the `sub-run-*` lifecycle markers; expose a `streamChild(childRunId)` API for opt-in subscription
options.description: Most surgical: parent stream stays small (just lifecycle), opt-in for inner detail. Pros: no event-shape changes, no envelope, consumers who don't care pay nothing. Cons: violates STREAM-01 wording ('emits each child event'); requires designing+exposing a new subscription API; harder to render a single timeline UI; breaks current `teedEmit` behavior callers may already depend on.

questions.title: How deep does the wrapping go for grandchildren and beyond?
questions.context: A coordinator at depth 0 delegates to a coordinator at depth 1 which delegates again to depth 2. The grandchild's events bubble up through depth-1's `teedEmit` to depth-0's stream. The wrapping needs a story for arbitrary depth (Phase 1 D-13 default `maxDepth=4`).

options.label: Always overwrite `parentRunId` to the IMMEDIATE parent — root stream sees only the top-level child id
options.description: Loses ancestry; consumer at root can demux by depth-1 child but can't distinguish depth-2 grandchildren of one child. Simplest implementation.

options.label: Build a `parentRunIds` chain (root → ... → immediate parent), each level prepends its runId
options.description: Full ancestry preserved. Root consumer can group by chain[0] (top-level child) or full chain (specific grandchild). Aligns with Q-01=c if chosen; if Q-01=a, this means upgrading `parentRunId` to a chain anyway.

options.label: Wrap envelopes recursively: `{ envelope, event: { envelope, event: <inner> } }`
options.description: Self-similar; each level wraps the lower level's envelope. Aligns with Q-01=b. Easy to walk recursively but adds nesting depth to every grandchild event.

options.label: Only depth-1 children bubble events to parent; depth-2+ events stay in their immediate parent's trace (no further bubbling)
options.description: Bounded blast radius — root never sees grandchild noise. Replay-parity friendly (traces are already nested). Cons: contradicts the 'live consumers see child events' UX goal.

questions.title: Per-child event-order preservation — is the existing `teedEmit` synchronous push enough?
questions.context: STREAM-02 mandates within-child order preservation, cross-child order unspecified. Current `teedEmit` (coordinator.ts:1118) pushes synchronously: `childEvents.push(event); options.emit?.(event)`. Multiple children dispatched in parallel via Phase 3's semaphore each have their own teedEmit closure. The parent stream's `publish()` (engine.ts:316) drains a single FIFO queue, so emissions are serialized at the parent level.

options.label: Existing synchronous teedEmit is sufficient — add a contract test asserting per-child order, no code changes to the emit path
options.description: JavaScript is single-threaded; sequential `await runProtocol(...)` per child guarantees in-child ordering. The parent stream's serial publisher means cross-child ordering is wall-clock interleave but per-child stays monotonic. Cheapest path.

options.label: Add an explicit per-child sequence number on bubbled events (`childSeq: number`), so consumers can verify/reorder
options.description: Belt-and-suspenders: even if a future refactor batches events, consumers can re-sort. Adds a public-surface field to every bubbled event. Slight overhead per event.

options.label: Refactor teedEmit to use a per-child queue drained by a single per-parent flush loop, formalize the ordering invariant
options.description: Introduces explicit machinery; makes the ordering invariant visible in code. Heavier change, possible perf trade. Probably overkill given (a)'s correctness.

questions.title: Does the wrapped event also land in the parent's RunResult.events trace?
questions.context: Today child events are NOT in the parent's `events` array — they live in `subRun.subResult.events`. The replay contract walks the embedded subResult. If we add `parentRunId` (Q-01=a) or wrap envelopes (Q-01=b) for the LIVE stream, the question is whether the parent's persisted `events` array gains those entries too. Phase 2 D-15 enforced parent-events isolation via budget-first-stop.test.ts.

options.label: Stream-only wrapping; parent's persisted `events` array is unchanged (child events stay in `subResult.events` only)
options.description: Preserves Phase 2 D-15's parent-events isolation invariant. Replay walks subResults as today. Live stream consumers see the wrapped events; persisted-trace consumers walk the recursive structure. CLEAN separation between live UX and replay shape.

options.label: Wrapped child events ALSO land in parent's `events` array (flattened bubbling)
options.description: Single timeline view for replay. Breaks D-15 isolation — termination math (Phase 2 D-15/16/17) needs explicit filtering by `parentRunId === self`. Replay round-trip gets weird (events appear in two places: parent's flat list AND child's nested subResult).

options.label: Add a separate `RunResult.streamLog?: readonly StreamEvent[]` field that mirrors what the live stream emitted
options.description: Doesn't pollute `events`; gives replay-from-stream a recoverable timeline. Significant new field on RunResult, doubles persistence cost. Probably overkill.

questions.title: Does `StreamHandle.cancel()` reuse the existing parent AbortController path, or add a stream-specific tear-down?
questions.context: Phase 2 D-07 already wires per-child AbortControllers that forward parent.signal abort to every in-flight child engine (coordinator.ts:1163-1178). engine.ts:293 already produces `createStreamCancellationError` on parent abort. STREAM-03 spec: 'StreamHandle.cancel() on the parent aborts every in-flight child stream and the parent stream itself with DogpileError({ code: aborted }).'

options.label: Reuse — `StreamHandle.cancel()` aborts the parent's signal; existing Phase 2 propagation handles every child engine; parent stream emits a final error event then closes
options.description: Most surgical. Phase 2 already shipped the per-child controller pattern specifically as 'lays groundwork for Phase 4 streaming.' Just verify the path end-to-end and add the streaming-side contract test in `streaming-api.test.ts`. Phase 3 D-17's commented hook is the attach point.

options.label: Add a separate `streamHandles: Set<StreamHandle>` on the per-child runtime context; cancel() walks and explicitly tears down each
options.description: Explicit ownership of stream handles. Useful if children expose their own StreamHandle (which today they don't — they're internal `runProtocol` calls). Heavier; only needed if Q-08 introduces caller-visible per-child handles.

questions.title: When the parent stream cancels, what's the LAST event the consumer sees?
questions.context: engine.ts:293-297 currently emits a `StreamErrorEvent` then `closeStream()`. Subscribers must observe a terminal event. With children involved, there could be in-flight `sub-run-*` events that haven't drained.

options.label: Single terminal `error` event with `code: aborted`, `detail.reason: parent-aborted` (or `timeout`); in-flight child events MAY arrive before it but never after
options.description: Matches existing behavior. Consumers know `error` = stream closed. In-flight child engines that haven't observed the abort yet may still emit a few events before they shut down — per-child controllers will rapid-fire abort. Pragmatic and already mostly true.

options.label: Synthetic `sub-run-failed` for every in-flight child (with `detail.reason: parent-aborted`), then the parent `error` event
options.description: Trace-completeness: every started child gets an explicit terminator before the parent error. Aligns with Phase 3 D-09's 'sibling-failed' synthetic event pattern. More events to manage but better replay parity.

options.label: Single terminal `error` event AND ensure no further `sub-run-*` events are emitted after cancel() returns
options.description: Strictest contract: hard-stop emission immediately on cancel. Implementation: gate `publish()` on a `closing` flag set synchronously by cancel(). May drop in-flight child events that arrive in the same microtask.

sections.title: Child Error Surfacing into Coordinator Decision (ERROR-01)

questions.title: How does the failed child appear in the coordinator's NEXT decision context?
questions.context: Today coordinator.ts:459 appends a synthetic transcript line: `[sub-run X failed]: <error.message>`. ERROR-01 spec: 'A child failure surfaces as a `sub-run-failed` event in the coordinator's next decision context; the coordinator agent can retry, delegate differently, or terminate.' The decision context = the prompt fed to the coordinator agent for its next plan-turn.

options.label: Keep the current taggedText synthetic transcript line — but enrich with structured fields (error code, partialCost, attempt#)
options.description: Minimal change. The coordinator agent reads the failure as text in the same transcript stream as successes. Predictable; replay-stable; fits Phase 1 D-18's transcript-entry pattern. Enrichment: include `error.code`, `error.detail.reason`, `partialCost.usd` so the agent has actionable data.

options.label: Add a structured `failures: ReadonlyArray<{childRunId, error, intent, partialCost}>` block in the coordinator prompt, separate from transcript
options.description: Explicit machine-readable failure roster in the prompt. Easier for the agent to enumerate. Requires changing the coordinator prompt template — public-ish surface (defines what the LLM sees). Risk: prompt drift across versions affects determinism.

options.label: Both — taggedText line for human-readable transcript continuity AND a structured failures section the planner formats before the model call
options.description: Belt-and-suspenders. Prompt grows but agent gets max signal. Planner judgment on prompt template. Higher token cost per failure.

questions.title: Should the coordinator agent be able to query the child's PARTIAL trace (not just the error message)?
questions.context: `SubRunFailedEvent` already carries `partialTrace` (Phase 2 D-09) — the events the child emitted before dying. Today this is for replay/observability, not exposed to the coordinator agent. ERROR-01 lets the agent 'retry, delegate differently' — it might want to see WHERE the child failed.

options.label: No — coordinator sees only `error.message`, `error.code`, `partialCost`; partialTrace is for the developer/replay only
options.description: Cheaper prompts. Coordinator decisions stay high-level (retry vs different intent vs terminate) without LLM weighing trace internals. Avoids token blow-up on failure-heavy runs.

options.label: Yes — surface a compact summary of partialTrace (e.g., last 3 events, truncated) in the failures block
options.description: More informed retries (e.g., 'child failed mid-tool-call after 2 successful turns'). Token cost per failure increases. Requires designing the summarization (deterministic for replay).

options.label: Defer — Phase 4 ships error.message + code + reason only; partialTrace surfacing is a Phase 5+ enhancement if requested
options.description: YAGNI posture. Phase 4 stays focused on the spec wording (sub-run-failed in decision context). Easy follow-up if real users request it.

questions.title: After surfacing the failure, what's the dispatch loop's default behavior?
questions.context: Coordinator.ts re-issues the plan turn after sub-run results. With a failure surfaced, the agent might respond with: (1) another delegate (retry/different), (2) participate (final synthesis), (3) nothing — i.e., the coordinator emits no decision. Need to decide what 'nothing' means.

options.label: Always re-issue plan turn after a failure; agent decides retry/different/terminate via decision (or `participate` for final answer)
options.description: Spec wording. Coordinator drives. If termination policy fires before the agent gets to respond, ERROR-02 kicks in (see Q-10). Pure agent-in-the-loop.

options.label: Add a runtime config `onChildFailure?: 'continue' | 'abort'` (default 'continue') — 'abort' skips re-issuing the plan turn and triggers ERROR-02 immediately
options.description: Caller-side escape hatch for fail-fast posture. Useful for batch jobs where one failure means abort. Public-surface addition. Default preserves spec behavior.

options.label: Always re-issue, but the agent's DecisionContext gets a hint flag `hasUnhandledFailure: true` so the prompt can include retry guidance
options.description: Implicit nudge to the agent. Spec-compliant. Slight prompt change. Test surface for the hint flag.

questions.title: What counts as 'unhandled child failure' that triggers the parent throw?
questions.context: ROADMAP success #4: 'If the parent terminates without final synthesis after an unhandled child failure, the parent throws the child's original DogpileError unwrapped.' Need to define the trigger condition precisely.

options.label: Parent run ends without a `final` event AND its events array contains at least one `sub-run-failed` → throw the FIRST failure's error
options.description: Simple invariant. 'Final synthesis' = the coordinator emitted a `final` event. Anything that ends the run before that (budget, termination floor, depth overflow) and has a failure in trace → re-throw. First failure preserves causal order.

options.label: Same condition, but throw the LAST `sub-run-failed`'s error (most recent failure)
options.description: Last-wins semantics. The most recent failure is closest to the termination decision; arguably more relevant for debugging. Inconsistent with 'original' wording ('throws the child's original DogpileError').

options.label: Track an explicit `acknowledged: boolean` per failure — set when the coordinator's next decision references the failed childRunId; throw the first UN-acknowledged failure on terminate
options.description: Most precise: only failures the agent never had a chance to handle (or chose to ignore) escalate. Heavier — needs acknowledgement tracking. Risk: ill-defined for free-text agent responses ('reference the childRunId' is fuzzy). Avoid unless needed.

options.label: Throw an aggregate error wrapping ALL un-handled child failures (`code: aborted`, `detail.failures: [...]`)
options.description: Multi-failure visibility. Violates 'original DogpileError unwrapped' spec wording. Useful for batch fan-outs but contradicts the requirement. Reject.

questions.title: What does 'unwrapped' mean in practice when re-throwing the child's error?
questions.context: Spec: 'throws the child's original DogpileError unwrapped (same code, providerId, detail).' The child's DogpileError is captured in `errorPayloadFromUnknown` (coordinator.ts:1244) and stored in `sub-run-failed.error`. We need to preserve identity vs reconstruct.

options.label: Hold a reference to the original `Error` instance in a Map keyed by childRunId; re-throw `instanceof DogpileError` verbatim on parent terminate
options.description: Strictest 'unwrapped': SAME object, .stack preserved, instance equality holds. Memory cost: hold error refs through the parent run's lifetime. Cleanest UX for catch blocks doing `instanceof`.

options.label: Reconstruct a new DogpileError from `sub-run-failed.error` payload (code, providerId, detail) — same fields, different instance
options.description: JSON-faithful. Replay produces an identical re-throw (replay only has the serialized payload). Loses .stack. instanceof DogpileError still true. Matches 'same code, providerId, detail' spec wording literally.

options.label: Hold reference at runtime (a) AND fall back to reconstruction (b) during replay
options.description: Best of both — runtime gets identity, replay still works. Two code paths, slightly more complex but small. Probably the right answer if (a)'s memory cost is acceptable.

questions.title: Which terminations count as 'parent terminates without final synthesis'?
questions.context: Termination paths today: (1) coordinator emits `final` (success), (2) budget timeout (`maxIterations`/`maxRounds`/`maxCost`/`timeoutMs`), (3) explicit cancellation (parent.signal.abort()), (4) depth overflow at dispatch, (5) coordinator emits no decision (degenerate plan-turn). All except (1) are 'no final synthesis.'

options.label: Every termination except (1) re-throws an unhandled child failure if one exists
options.description: Broadest coverage. Cancellation while a child failure is unacknowledged → throw the child's error not the cancel error. Risk: cancel-after-failure gets the failure error, masking the cancel intent. Could be confusing.

options.label: Only budget-driven and degenerate-plan-turn terminations re-throw; explicit cancellation throws the cancel error verbatim
options.description: Cancel intent always wins. User who called `.cancel()` gets the abort error, not a child failure. Aligns with principle of least surprise for cancel callers. Safer default.

options.label: Only degenerate plan-turn (coordinator emitted no decision while a failure exists) — narrow definition
options.description: Most conservative. Budget terminations get the budget error (Phase 2 D-15 territory); cancels get cancel errors. Only the 'agent gave up silently' path escalates. Aligns with 'unhandled' as 'agent didn't address it.'

questions.title: What sources today produce `code: provider-timeout` at the child level?
questions.context: ROADMAP success #5: 'Child timeouts surface as `provider-timeout` at the child level; parent-level timeouts surface as `aborted` with `detail.reason: timeout`.' Phase 2 D-08/D-13 already lock parent-side `aborted` + `detail.reason: timeout`. The child side: a child's OWN deadline (decision-supplied or inherited via Phase 2 D-11/12) expiring → ?

options.label: Child engine's OWN budget.timeoutMs firing → `code: provider-timeout` (re-classify the abort path inside the child)
options.description: Cleanest model: a child whose own budget expires self-classifies as `provider-timeout` (the child's perspective). Only the PARENT's budget propagating down (Phase 2 D-11 deadline math) keeps the `aborted+timeout` shape. Requires distinguishing 'my own deadline' vs 'inherited deadline.'

options.label: Provider call timeouts (the underlying HTTP/SDK timeout) → `code: provider-timeout`; ALL Dogpile-level deadlines → `code: aborted, reason: timeout`
options.description: Strict layering: `provider-timeout` is reserved for actual provider-call timeouts (already a thing in error vocabulary). Engine deadlines always abort. Aligns with the existing error-code vocabulary; no re-classification needed for engine paths.

options.label: (a) AND (b) — distinguish three: provider HTTP timeout (`provider-timeout`), child's own engine budget (`provider-timeout` w/ `detail.source: engine`), parent budget propagated (`aborted+timeout`)
options.description: Most discriminating. Three observable cases. Adds a `detail.source` field to disambiguate the two `provider-timeout` flavors. Slight surface inflation; max diagnostic clarity.

questions.title: Where does the timeout-classification logic live?
questions.context: Today `enrichAbortErrorWithParentReason` (coordinator.ts:1243) tags abort errors with `parent-aborted` vs `timeout` based on the PARENT's signal reason. Child-level classification needs a hook somewhere.

options.label: Inside the child engine's abort lifecycle (engine.ts) — child engine inspects whether the abort reason is its own timeout vs an inherited signal
options.description: Symmetric: every engine self-classifies. Reusable for nested coordinators. Touches engine.ts cancellation paths. Cleanest separation.

options.label: In `dispatchDelegate` (coordinator.ts) — the dispatcher knows whether the timeout came from `decision.budget.timeoutMs` (child's own) or parent's deadline propagation, and re-classifies on the way out
options.description: Localizes the change to coordinator.ts where Phase 2 D-11/12 already work. Engine stays unaware. Tighter blast radius.

options.label: In `cancellation.ts` helpers — the existing `enrichAbortErrorWithParentReason` is extended with a sibling helper for child-level classification, called from both engine.ts and coordinator.ts
options.description: Helper-driven; logic in one place, called from both layers as needed. Mirrors Phase 2 D-08's pattern.

questions.title: Public-surface inventory for Phase 4 — what locks together?
questions.context: Per CLAUDE.md, every event/result/exports change updates `event-schema.test.ts`, `result-contract.test.ts`, `package-exports.test.ts`, `package.json` exports/files, and CHANGELOG.md. Phase 4 candidates depend on Q-01/Q-09/Q-10/Q-13 answers but the inventory needs to be enumerated.

options.label: Minimal: `parentRunId` (or chain) on bubbled events + ERROR-02 throw behavior + ERROR-03 reason discriminator strings
options.description: Smallest plausible surface. No new event variants, no new error codes (reuse existing `aborted`+`provider-timeout`). Only test additions and a new `detail.reason` value if Q-13 introduces one. CHANGELOG bullets: streaming wrap, ERROR-02 throw, ERROR-03 timeout discrimination.

options.label: Minimal + new `aborted` event variant from Phase 2 D-10 (parent-aborted-after-child-completed) — finally land the deferred shape
options.description: Closes the Phase 2 D-10 'planner picks shape' open item. New event variant in `event-schema.test.ts`. Coherent with the streaming/cancel work. Bigger surface.

options.label: Minimal + new `onChildFailure` engine config option (if Q-09=b)
options.description: Conditional on Q-09=b. Adds engine config plumbing similar to Phase 3's `maxConcurrentChildren`. `config-validation.test.ts` updates.

options.label: All of the above — Phase 4 is the kitchen-sink phase before Phase 5 docs
options.description: Maximum scope. Higher risk of plan churn. Aligns with 'last functional phase before docs' positioning if we want everything locked before v0.4.0 ships.

questions.title: Test organization for Phase 4 — same hybrid pattern as Phase 2/3?
questions.context: Phase 2 D-18 / Phase 3 D-15 established hybrid: contract tests in dedicated files (`cancellation-contract.test.ts`, `event-schema.test.ts`), scenario tests in subject-adjacent files (`coordinator.test.ts`, `engine.test.ts`).

options.label: Hybrid, same pattern. Streaming-wrap contract → `streaming-api.test.ts`. ERROR-02 throw → `public-error-api.test.ts`. ERROR-03 timeout discriminator → `cancellation-contract.test.ts`. Scenarios → `coordinator.test.ts`, `engine.test.ts`
options.description: Continuity with Phase 2/3. Files already exist (per ROADMAP key files for Phase 4). Lowest cognitive load.

options.label: Same as (a) but extract a new `src/tests/recursive-coordination.test.ts` for cross-cutting end-to-end scenarios that touch streaming + error + concurrency
options.description: End-to-end home for the v0.4.0 milestone showcase scenarios. Useful for the Phase 5 example to mirror. Adds a new test file.

options.label: Promotion-on-growth: start in existing files, extract new files only if any single concern grows past ~150 LOC
options.description: Phase 3 D-15's policy. Defers the file-organization question to plan time.

questions.title: Plan breakdown for Phase 4 — how many plans and what's the dependency order?
questions.context: Phase 2 = 4 plans (one per BUDGET req). Phase 3 = 3 plans (grouped by concern). Phase 4 has 6 requirements (STREAM-01/02/03 + ERROR-01/02/03). Some couple naturally (STREAM-01+02 → wrapping; STREAM-03 → cancel; ERROR-01 → context surfacing; ERROR-02 → throw; ERROR-03 → timeout vocab).

options.label: 3 plans: 04-01 Streaming (STREAM-01/02/03 — wrapping + cancel propagation), 04-02 Error escalation in coordinator (ERROR-01), 04-03 Unhandled throw + timeout discrimination (ERROR-02 + ERROR-03)
options.description: Concern-grouped, mirrors Phase 3 (3 plans). Each plan has surgical scope. ERROR-02 and ERROR-03 share the abort/error vocabulary work so co-locating them helps. Dependency order: 04-01 → 04-02 → 04-03 (cancel work in 04-01 is foundation for 04-02's error context which feeds 04-03's throw).

options.label: 2 plans: 04-01 Streaming (STREAM-01/02/03), 04-02 Errors (ERROR-01/02/03)
options.description: Coarse split. Easier to track but each plan is larger. ERROR-01/02/03 share enough machinery to merge cleanly. Risk: 04-02 grows large.

options.label: 6 plans, one per requirement
options.description: Most granular; matches Phase 2's discipline. Risk: ERROR-02 alone is a 30-line plan (overkill); ERROR-03 mostly already shipped in Phase 2. Some plans become trivial.

options.label: 4 plans: 04-01 Stream wrapping (STREAM-01/02), 04-02 Cancel propagation (STREAM-03), 04-03 Error context (ERROR-01), 04-04 Throw + timeout (ERROR-02/03)
options.description: Splits streaming into two — wrapping vs cancel — for cleaner reviews. Cancel work touches engine.ts heavily; isolating it might help. 4 plans matches Phase 2's count.
